content:
RAG CHATBOT PROJECT 
COMPLETE INTERVIEW PREPARATION SHEET  v2 
Fresher Level  •  59 Questions  •  WHY questions + Model Answers  •  Score Sheet 
Python  •  LangChain  •  LlamaIndex  •  FAISS  •  FastAPI  •  Streamlit  •  Docker  
 
Blue Q  — concept / implementation Orange WHY Q  — design decision / 
trade-off 
Green box = ideal answer for every 
question 
 
Section 1 — Project Overview 
Big picture understanding — communication + architecture

Q1 Walk me through this project in 60 seconds as if explaining to a non-technical person. 
[Communication] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
I built a chatbot that lets you upload any document — PDF, Word file, CSV, or a webpage link — and ask questions 
about it. Unlike ChatGPT, it only answers from your actual document and shows you which page the answer came 
from. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q2 What problem does RAG solve that plain ChatGPT cannot? 
[Concept]

[Concept] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Plain GPT has a knowledge cutoff and no access to private data. RAG retrieves relevant chunks from your own 
documents before generating, grounding every answer in real content and eliminating hallucinations about your 
data. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q3 
WHY? 
Why build a RAG chatbot instead of a simpler project? 
vs: simple chatbot vs RAG 
IDEAL 
ANSWER

vs: simple chatbot vs RAG 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
RAG combines file handling, vector databases, API design, LLMs, memory, and frontend deployment — more skills 
demonstrated in one project than a basic CRUD app. It also solves a real business problem companies pay for. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q4 What is the complete data flow from file upload to answer? 
[Architecture] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER

[Architecture] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Upload → format detection (PDF/DOCX/CSV/URL) → format-aware chunking → OpenAI embeddings → FAISS 
index merge → user question → embed question → FAISS top-k retrieval → chunks + chat history stuffed into 
prompt → GPT generates answer → answer + source citations returned to UI. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________

Q5 Which day did you find hardest and how did you debug it? 
[Debugging] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Day 10 — FAISS merge_from() silently dropped metadata when indexes had different field structures. I debugged 
by printing chunk metadata after merge and found the enrichment pipeline wasn't running on one loader. Fixed by 
routing all loaders through the same metadata.py step. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
 
 
 
Section 2 — Embeddings & Vector Search

Section 2 — Embeddings & Vector Search 
Day 2–4 — expect the deepest questions here 
 
Q6 What is an embedding and what does text-embedding-3-small output? 
[Concept] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
An embedding converts text into a list of ~1536 floating-point numbers representing semantic meaning. Similar 
sentences produce vectors that point in similar directions in this high-dimensional space, enabling mathematical 
similarity comparison. 
Score: 
___ /5

similarity comparison. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q7 
WHY? 
Why FAISS instead of ChromaDB? 
vs: FAISS vs ChromaDB vs Pinecone  
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
FAISS is a pure in-memory C++ library — zero server setup, extremely fast, just two files on disk. ChromaDB needs 
a running server and adds metadata filtering but that complexity isn't needed for a single-user demo. I'd switch to 
ChromaDB or Pinecone for multi-user apps needing filtering. 
Score: 
___ /5

Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q8 
WHY? 
Why text-embedding-3-small instead of ada-002 or 3-large? 
vs: 3-small vs ada-002 vs 3-large 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
text-embedding-3-small outperforms ada-002 on most benchmarks at lower cost — it's the best accuracy-to-price 
ratio. 3-large is ~13% more accurate but costs 5x more per token. For a portfolio demo, small is the smart default. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________

Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q9 What is cosine similarity and why use it for text? 
[Concept] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors — not size but direction. A long sentence and a short 
sentence saying the same thing have different magnitudes but point the same direction, so cosine gives them a high 
similarity score. Perfect for semantic text matching. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________

Q10 
WHY? 
Why k=4 in similarity_search instead of k=1 or k=20? 
vs: k=4 vs k=1 vs k=20 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
k=1 misses context spread across multiple paragraphs. k=20 floods the prompt with irrelevant content — the LLM 
gets confused (lost-in-the-middle problem) and token cost spikes. k=4 with 500-char chunks fits ~2000 chars of 
context, enough for most answers without noise. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q11 
WHY?

Q11 
WHY? 
Why persist the FAISS index to disk instead of rebuilding on every startup? 
vs: rebuild every time vs persist 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Each embedding call costs money. A 100-page PDF produces ~400 chunks = 400 API calls every restart. Saving 
index.faiss and index.pkl means embed once, reload instantly forever. The two files together reconstruct the full 
index including text and metadata. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________

Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q12 If the user asks something not in the document, what does FAISS return? 
[Edge Cases] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
FAISS always returns k results regardless of relevance — it finds the least-dissimilar chunks. The system prompt 
handles this: 'If the answer is not in the context, say I don't have that information.' This prevents the LLM from 
hallucinating an answer from its training data. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________

Section 3 — Document Ingestion & Chunking 
Day 3, 8–9 — format-aware splitting pipeline 
 
Q13 
WHY? 
Why chunk documents instead of embedding the whole file? 
vs: whole file vs chunks 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
LLMs have context window limits (GPT-3.5 = 16k tokens). A 200-page PDF doesn't fit in one prompt. Also, one 
embedding for a whole document loses precision — a question about page 50 is drowned by signal from pages 1–
200. Smaller chunks = sharper, more accurate retrieval. 
Score: 
___ /5

Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q14 
WHY? 
Why RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter instead of CharacterTextSplitter? 
vs: Recursive vs Character splitter  
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
CharacterTextSplitter splits on one separator — if that separator is rare, chunks can be huge. 
RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter tries \n\n then \n then . then space in order, always finding a natural break. It never 
cuts mid-sentence, preserving readable, meaningful chunks. 
Score: 
___ /5

Q15 What is chunk_overlap and what happens if you set it to 0? 
[Parameter Tuning] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Overlap copies the last N characters from one chunk into the start of the next. Without overlap, a key sentence split 
across a boundary is half in chunk 3 (no context) and half in chunk 4 (no context). 50-char overlap ensures the LLM 
sees the full sentence in at least one chunk. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q16 
WHY?

Q16 
WHY? 
Why use different chunk sizes per format (PDF=500, CSV=800, URL=400)? 
vs: format-aware vs uniform chunking 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
PDFs have structured paragraphs — 500 chars preserves them intact. CSV rows are atomic facts — 800 groups a 
few rows for related context. URLs are noisy with nav/footer junk — 400 keeps only content. Uniform chunking 
would hurt retrieval quality across formats. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q17 
WHY?

Q17 
WHY? 
Why PyPDFLoader instead of pdfplumber or PyMuPDF? 
vs: PyPDFLoader vs pdfplumber vs PyMuPDF  
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
PyPDFLoader is native LangChain — outputs Document objects with page metadata automatically, plugs directly 
into the pipeline. pdfplumber handles tables better but adds complexity. PyMuPDF is faster for large files but needs 
extra system dependencies. For a demo, simplicity wins. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________

Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q18 What metadata does each chunk carry and why does it matter for the user? 
[Metadata] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
source_type, filename, page number, chunk_index, chunk_hash, char_count, ingested_at. Page number lets users 
verify the answer in the original document. chunk_hash enables deduplication. filename is shown in source citations 
so users know which document the answer came from. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________

Q19 
WHY? 
Why WebBaseLoader instead of requests + BeautifulSoup directly? 
vs: WebBaseLoader vs raw scraping  
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
WebBaseLoader returns LangChain Document objects with metadata already populated — it slots directly into the 
splitter pipeline with no wrapping code. Raw BeautifulSoup needs manual Document construction. For JS-heavy 
sites I'd upgrade to Playwright. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q20 How does a CSV file become searchable chunks?

[CSV Ingestion] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Each row is converted to a text string: 'column_name: value' for every column, joined by newlines — making it 
readable English. These strings become Documents and go through the standard splitter and embedding pipeline. A 
query about a product price matches the row containing that product. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________

Section 4 — RAG Chain & LLM 
Day 5–6 — chain types, prompting, memory 
 
Q21 What are the three steps of RAG? 
[Concept] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Retrieve: embed the question and find the k most similar chunks in FAISS. Augment: combine those chunks with the 
question and chat history into a single prompt. Generate: the LLM reads only that prompt and produces a grounded 
answer with source references. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q22 
WHY?

Q22 
WHY? 
Why chain_type='stuff' instead of map_reduce or refine? 
vs: stuff vs map_reduce vs refine 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Stuff pastes all retrieved chunks directly into one prompt — simplest and fastest. With k=4 chunks of 500 chars 
each, the total context is ~2000 chars, well within GPT-3.5's 16k token limit. map_reduce is needed for 100+ 
chunks. refine is more accurate but makes multiple LLM calls, costing more. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q23 
WHY?

Q23 
WHY? 
Why temperature=0 instead of 0.7? 
vs: temperature=0 vs 0.7 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
temperature=0 makes GPT fully deterministic — it always picks the highest-probability token. For factual document 
Q&A, we want consistent, reproducible answers, not creative variation. temperature=0.7 would risk the LLM 
embellishing or contradicting the source document. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q24 
WHY? 
Why switch to ConversationalRetrievalChain on Day 6?

vs: ConversationalRetrievalChain vs RetrievalQA  
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
RetrievalQA has no memory — every question is independent. If the user asks 'What is it about?' then 'Tell me more 
about that,' the second question has no referent for 'that.' ConversationalRetrievalChain reformulates follow-ups 
using chat history before retrieving, making multi-turn conversation work naturally. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q25 
WHY?

Q25 
WHY? 
Why window memory of 3 exchanges instead of keeping all history? 
vs: window=3 vs unlimited memory  
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Each stored exchange adds tokens to every subsequent prompt. Unlimited memory causes prompts to grow until 
they hit the context limit and the API errors. 3 exchanges covers typical conversational depth while keeping prompt 
size and cost bounded and predictable. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________

Q26 How does your system prompt prevent hallucination? 
[Prompt Engineering] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Three rules in the system prompt: (1) Use ONLY the context below — prevents drawing from training data. (2) If not 
in context, say 'I don't have that information in the document' — explicit fallback phrase. (3) Cite which document 
each fact comes from — adds accountability and verifiability. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
 
 
 
Section 5 — FastAPI Backend

Section 5 — FastAPI Backend 
Day 7–8 — API design, validation, error handling 
 
Q27 
WHY? 
Why FastAPI instead of Flask? 
vs: FastAPI vs Flask vs Django 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
FastAPI is async by default — LLM calls can take 3–5 seconds and async prevents blocking other requests. It auto-
generates Swagger docs at /docs with zero code. Pydantic validation is built in — Flask needs marshmallow or 
manual checks. For an AI API, FastAPI is the industry standard. 
Score: 
___ /5

Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q28 
WHY? 
Why separate /upload and /upload/url endpoints? 
vs: one endpoint vs separate 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
File upload uses multipart/form-data (binary). URL ingestion uses a JSON body. These are different HTTP content 
types that can't cleanly share one endpoint. Separate endpoints give clear validation, error messages, and OpenAPI 
docs for each case. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________

Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q29 What is a Pydantic model and how did you use it? 
[Pydantic] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Pydantic validates data types automatically. ChatRequest ensures 'question' is a non-empty string. ChatResponse 
guarantees the response has 'answer' (string) and 'sources' (list). If the client sends wrong data types, FastAPI 
returns a 422 error before any code runs. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________

Q30 What does CORSMiddleware do and why did you need it? 
[Security]  [CORS] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
CORS is a browser security rule that blocks requests from a different origin (domain+port). Streamlit runs on port 
8501, FastAPI on 8000 — different origins. Without CORSMiddleware, the browser rejects every Streamlit call to the 
API before it even reaches FastAPI. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________

Q31 
WHY? 
Why a global chat_chain variable instead of creating a new chain per request? 
vs: global vs per-request chain 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Building the chain loads the FAISS index and initialises the LLM — adds 1–2 seconds per message. The global 
chain initialises once on first request and is reused. Trade-off: not thread-safe for multiple simultaneous users — in 
production I'd use a per-session chain pool. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________

Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q32 How would you handle a 500MB PDF upload gracefully? 
[Scalability] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Add file size validation in the endpoint — return 413 if over limit. Move processing to a BackgroundTask so the 
HTTP response returns immediately. Return a job_id the client polls via GET /status/{job_id}. For very large indexes, 
switch from in-memory FAISS to a disk-based solution like FAISS with mmap. 
Score: 
___ /5

Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
 
 
 
Section 6 — Multi-Document Support 
Day 10 — FAISS merging, registry 
 
Q33 
WHY? 
Why merge_from() instead of rebuilding the index on each upload? 
vs: merge_from() vs full rebuild 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Rebuilding re-embeds every previously uploaded document — wasting API calls proportional to total documents. 
merge_from() merges a new small index into the existing one in milliseconds with no re-embedding. Trade-off:

individual documents can't be removed without a full rebuild. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q34 What is registry.json and why is it needed? 
[Data Management] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
A JSON file tracking each document's name, source type, chunk count, and ingestion timestamp. Without it there's 
no way to know what's inside the FAISS index — the index is a black box of vectors. The registry powers the 
/documents endpoint and the sidebar document list. 
Score: 
___ /5

Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q35 If two documents contradict each other, what does the chatbot say? 
[Edge Cases] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
FAISS returns the most similar chunks from both. The LLM receives context from both and the system prompt 
instructs it to cite each source. A good LLM will note the disagreement: 'doc1.pdf states X while the Wikipedia article 
states Y.' The user can then judge. 
Score: 
___ /5

Q36 
WHY? 
Why keep uploaded files on disk instead of deleting after processing? 
vs: keep files vs discard 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Files enable re-ingestion without re-upload after a server restart, auditing of what data was ingested, and debugging 
— if a chunk looks wrong, you open the original. Trade-off: disk usage grows. The /reset endpoint clears both the 
index and the uploads folder. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
 
 
 
Section 7 — LangChain vs LlamaIndex

Section 7 — LangChain vs LlamaIndex 
Day 11 — framework comparison 
 
Q37 
WHY? 
Why LangChain as default and LlamaIndex as optional? 
vs: LangChain vs LlamaIndex as primary  
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
LangChain has built-in ConversationBufferWindowMemory, full custom prompt control, and clean FastAPI 
integration. LlamaIndex abstracts too much — injecting a custom system prompt requires fighting the framework. 
For an API-first chatbot, LangChain's explicit pipeline is the better fit. 
Score:

Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
 
Feature LangChain LlamaIndex 
Setup complexity Medium — more code Low — SimpleDirectoryReader 
Custom prompts Full control Limited by abstraction 
Memory Built-in Manual implementation 
FastAPI fit Excellent Good 
Advanced retrieval Good Sub-question + recursive 
Best for APIs + pipelines Document Q&A prototypes 
 
Q38 How did you switch backends at runtime without restarting the API? 
[API Design] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER

[API Design] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Added ?backend=llamaindex as a query parameter to the /chat endpoint. The endpoint checks this parameter: if 
'llamaindex', it loads the LlamaIndex VectorStoreIndex and calls query_engine.query(). Otherwise it uses the default 
LangChain chain. No restart, no state change needed. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________

Section 8 — Streamlit Frontend 
Day 12 — UI, session state, API integration 
 
Q39 
WHY? 
Why Streamlit instead of React or Vue? 
vs: Streamlit vs React/Vue 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Streamlit lets a Python developer build a working UI in hours without learning JavaScript. For a portfolio project 
demonstrating RAG skills, the AI pipeline matters more than the UI framework. In production with many users, React 
would be better for performance and real-time streaming. 
Score: 
___ /5

Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q40 How does chat history persist between user messages in Streamlit? 
[Session State] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Streamlit reruns the entire Python script on every interaction. st.session_state is a persistent dictionary that survives 
reruns. I store the messages list in session_state — each rerun reads it and re-renders the full conversation from 
top to bottom. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q41 
WHY?

Q41 
WHY? 
Why read API_URL from an environment variable? 
vs: env var vs hardcoded URL 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
In Docker, the frontend container can't reach the backend via 'localhost' — it must use the Docker service name 
'backend'. The environment variable lets the same Streamlit code work locally (localhost:8000) and in Docker 
(backend:8000) without any code change. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q42 How do source citations appear in the Streamlit UI? 
[UI/UX]

[UI/UX] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
After each assistant message, I render a collapsible st.expander showing each source chunk with its filename, page 
number, and first 200 characters of content. This lets the user verify the answer against the original document 
without leaving the app. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
 
 
 
Section 9 — Docker & Deployment 
Day 13–14 — containers, networking, HF Spaces 
 
Q43 
WHY?

Q43 
WHY? 
Why Docker instead of just sharing requirements.txt? 
vs: Docker vs requirements.txt only

IDEAL 
ANSWER 
requirements.txt only captures Python packages — not the Python version, OS libraries, system dependencies, or 
runtime environment. Docker freezes the entire runtime. Anyone runs 'docker-compose up --build' and gets the 
identical environment regardless of OS or existing Python installations. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q44 
WHY? 
Why two containers instead of running FastAPI and Streamlit in one? 
vs: two containers vs monolith 
IDEAL 
ANSWER

vs: two containers vs monolith 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Separation of concerns: if the UI crashes, the API keeps serving. Each service scales independently. If 1000 users 
hit the frontend and 10 hit the API, you can scale containers separately. It also mirrors real production architecture. 
Trade-off: requires Docker networking config. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q45 Why can't the frontend use 'localhost' to reach the backend in Docker? 
[Docker Networking] 
IDEAL

[Docker Networking] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Each container has its own network namespace — localhost inside the frontend container refers to that container 
only, not the host machine. Docker Compose creates a virtual bridge network where containers reach each other by 
service name. 'backend:8000' is the correct address. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q46 
WHY? 
Why mount faiss_index as a Docker volume? 
vs: volumes vs bake into image 
IDEAL 
ANSWER

vs: volumes vs bake into image 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
The FAISS index is generated at runtime from user uploads — it doesn't exist at build time and changes with every 
upload. A volume binds a host directory into the container so data survives container restarts and image rebuilds. 
Without it, every restart loses all uploaded documents. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q47 How did you store the OpenAI API key securely in Docker and on Hugging Face? 
[Security] 
IDEAL

[Security] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Locally: env_file: - .env in docker-compose.yml reads from a .env file that is git-ignored (never committed). On HF 
Spaces: added OPENAI_API_KEY as a Repository Secret in Settings — HF injects it as an environment variable. 
The key never appears in code or git history. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q48 
WHY? 
Why a separate app.py at root for HF Spaces instead of using frontend/app.py directly?

vs: HF Spaces single-process constraint 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
HF Spaces expects a single app.py entry point and provides only one process. Our project needs both FastAPI (port 
8000) and Streamlit (port 8501) running simultaneously. app.py starts the FastAPI server in a background thread, 
waits 4 seconds, then launches Streamlit — two services, one process. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q49 What is a Docker health check and how did you configure it?
[DevOps]  [Reliability]

IDEAL 
ANSWER 
A command Docker runs periodically to verify the container is working. I configured 'curl -f localhost:8000/health' 
every 30 seconds. After 3 consecutive failures, Docker marks the container unhealthy. The frontend's 'depends_on: 
condition: service_healthy' ensures Streamlit only starts after a healthy backend. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
 
 
 
Section 10 — Debugging & Problem Solving 
Real-world scenarios the interviewer will probe

Q50 The chatbot gives a wrong answer. Walk me through debugging it step by step. 
[Debugging] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Step 1: print log_retrieved_chunks() — are the right chunks being fetched? Step 2: If wrong chunks — check chunk 
size, overlap, embedding model, or k value. Step 3: If right chunks but wrong answer — check system prompt 
wording and temperature. Step 4: If empty results — verify FAISS index was built and saved correctly. 
Score: 
___ /5

Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q51 A user says the answer is on page 5 but the bot says it doesn't know. What happened? 
[Debugging]  [Retrieval] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
The key sentence was probably split across a chunk boundary, or the chunk was shorter than min_chars and got 
filtered out, or k=4 didn't reach the relevant chunk. Fix: increase k, reduce min_chars threshold, or increase 
chunk_overlap so boundary sentences appear in at least one complete chunk.

Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q52 
WHY? 
Why not set k=20 to always retrieve more chunks? 
vs: k=4 vs k=20 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
More chunks = longer prompt = higher token cost + slower response. Beyond k=6-8, the LLM suffers from 'lost in 
the middle' — it ignores chunks in the middle of a long context. The optimal k is the smallest value that reliably 
retrieves the relevant chunk for your chunk size. 
Score: 
___ /5

Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q53 A user uploads a scanned PDF with no text — what goes wrong and how would you fix it? 
[Edge Cases]  [OCR] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
PyPDFLoader returns empty page strings since it extracts text layer only. Fix: detect empty pages after loading, 
then run OCR on page images using pytesseract or send to AWS Textract. I'd add a check in pdf_loader.py: if 
len(page.page_content.strip()) == 0, flag for OCR processing. 
Score: 
___ /5

Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q54 
WHY? 
Why not use pgvector or Pinecone instead of FAISS for production? 
vs: FAISS vs pgvector vs Pinecone (production)

IDEAL 
ANSWER 
FAISS is perfect for single-user demos — zero infrastructure, just two files. pgvector adds SQL + vector search in 
one database, enabling filtering by user/date and multi-user isolation, but requires a PostgreSQL server. Pinecone is 
fully managed but costs money. Migrate when you need multi-user or metadata filtering. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q55 How would you add multi-user support so each user's documents are private?

[Scalability]  [Architecture] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Assign each user a session_id (UUID) at login. Store a separate FAISS index per user at faiss_index/{session_id}/. 
The /upload and /chat endpoints accept session_id and load the correct index. ConversationBufferWindowMemory 
is stored in a dict keyed by session_id and cleared on logout. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________  
 
Q56 If you had two more weeks, what would you improve first? 
[Product Thinking] 
IDEAL

[Product Thinking] 
IDEAL 
ANSWER 
Streaming responses with FastAPI StreamingResponse and Streamlit st.write_stream — so answers appear token 
by token instead of after a full wait. Second: user authentication with JWT. These two changes make it feel like a 
real commercial product rather than a demo. 
Score: 
___ /5 
Red flag:  Notes: ________________________________
